items.myth: ReLU has no vanishing-gradient problem because its derivative can be 1.
items.reality: ReLU avoids saturation on the positive branch, but when x ≤ 0 the derivative is 0—dead neurons pass zero gradient, which is a form of vanishing gradient localized to those units.
items.example: A neuron with z ≤ 0 for all batch samples has ∂L/∂w = 0; no amount of depth helps that unit learn until pre-activations cross zero.

items.myth: Leaky ReLU and PReLU are the same thing.
items.reality: Both allow negative slopes, but Leaky ReLU uses a fixed α set by the practitioner; PReLU treats α as a learnable parameter updated during training.
items.example: `nn.LeakyReLU(0.01)` always uses 0.01; `nn.PReLU(64).weight` changes every epoch as the optimizer tunes per-channel slopes.

items.myth: ReLU output is zero-centered like tanh.
items.reality: ReLU outputs are always non-negative; only the negative inputs are mapped to zero, so activations are not centered around zero.
items.example: Hidden layer after ReLU on random z: mean activation is positive; tanh on the same z would cluster around 0.

items.myth: More ReLU layers always improve expressiveness without downside.
items.reality: Each ReLU can zero out information; very deep stacks without skip connections or normalization increase dead-neuron risk and optimization difficulty.
items.example: A 20-layer plain ReLU MLP without batch norm may train worse than a 8-layer one with the same width—depth alone is not free capacity.
